metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `Retry-After` is extracted from ACLI subprocess output (e.g., regex on `cpe.stderr`, a known ACLI JSON field, or fallback to pure backoff) — the REST-path mechanism (`exc.headers`) does not exist on a `CalledProcessError` and cannot be ported directly.
findings.finding: The plan says 'parse/honor Retry-After' on the bulk path, but ACLI runs as a subprocess — the HTTP Retry-After response header is not available as a Python object. The REST path reads it from exc.headers on urllib.error.HTTPError (an in-process object); _run_acli has no such object. The header is only accessible if ACLI echoes it in its stderr/stdout text, which is not guaranteed and is not specified. The plan assumes the same mechanism works in both contexts, but it does not.
findings.location: ## What / files — 'parse/honor Retry-After'; AC line 'Bulk-path retries honor Retry-After on 429'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the spec: 'Extract Retry-After from cpe.stderr via regex (e.g. `re.search(r"Retry-After:\s*(\d+)", cpe.stderr)`) if ACLI echoes it; if not reliably present, document that Retry-After is best-effort and the implementation falls back to capped exponential backoff + jitter, which still satisfies the spirit of parity.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document (in the plan) how the ACLI Go binary signals a Jira 429 to the subprocess caller — specifically: what exit code it uses, and whether/where it prints the Retry-After value — before specifying the parse/honor-Retry-After implementation.
findings.finding: The plan assumes ACLI's subprocess output on a 429 response contains the Retry-After header value in a parseable form, but no evidence exists in the codebase that the ACLI Go binary surfaces HTTP headers (including Retry-After) in its stdout/stderr, nor that it exits with returncode 429 when Jira rate-limits it.
findings.location: ## What / files — `acli_subprocess.py` / AC: 'Bulk-path retries honor Retry-After on 429'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a spike/probe note to the plan: run `acli jira workitem search ...` against a rate-limited endpoint and capture the exit code and stderr/stdout to confirm the signal shape ACLI emits on 429. Only then can the plan specify whether to parse returncode, stderr regex, or a structured field.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the mechanism by which `Retry-After` from a Jira HTTP 429 response reaches `_run_acli` (e.g., ACLI Go echoes the header value in its stderr/stdout on rate-limit exit), or revise AC1 to 'jittered exponential backoff on 429 exit code' if `Retry-After` is not extractable from the subprocess interface.
findings.finding: The plan's premise that the 'bulk path' suffers from not parsing `Retry-After` assumes ACLI's subprocess calls surface HTTP 429 in a way that makes `Retry-After` header parsing possible. `_run_acli` only sees `subprocess.CalledProcessError` (exit codes) or `subprocess.TimeoutExpired` — it never sees `urllib.error.HTTPError` with headers. The plan does not specify how a `Retry-After` header emitted by Jira's HTTP layer would be extracted from ACLI's stdout/stderr/exit-code interface.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC1: 'Bulk-path retries honor Retry-After on 429 with jittered exponential backoff'; What/files section: 'parse/honor Retry-After'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (via ACLI Go docs or a probe run) whether the ACLI CLI binary emits the `Retry-After` header value in its stderr/stdout on a 429 response, and document the exact text format; if it does not, scope AC1 to 'jittered exponential backoff on 429' rather than claiming Retry-After is honored.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the bulk path will 'parse/honor Retry-After' on 429, but this is an unverified capability claim: _run_acli only receives subprocess CalledProcessError with text stdout/stderr, not a urllib.error.HTTPError with a .headers dict. Whether the ACLI Go binary actually emits the Retry-After header value in its CLI text output (and in what format) is nowhere established in the codebase, tests, or plan.
findings.location: ## What / files — 'parse/honor Retry-After' claim; AC line 'Bulk-path retries honor Retry-After on 429 with jittered exponential backoff.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before implementing, verify (against `acli jira workitem search --help` / ACLI changelog / a live or staged probe) that: (a) ACLI exits with returncode 429 when Jira rate-limits it, and (b) ACLI includes the Retry-After header value in its stderr/stdout output in a parseable form; if (b) is false, revise AC1 to 'honor Retry-After when ACLI surfaces it' or drop the Retry-After clause for the bulk path.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that Retry-After can be parsed from ACLI subprocess output on a 429, but ACLI is a JVM subprocess — Python only sees returncode + text (stdout/stderr). Whether ACLI (a) exits with returncode 429 specifically when rate-limited, and (b) emits the Retry-After header value in its stderr/stdout in a parseable form, are both unverified against ACLI --help or release notes. If ACLI uses a generic non-zero exit or does not surface Retry-After textually, AC1 ('honor Retry-After on 429') is not achievable as described.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — AC1: 'Bulk-path retries honor Retry-After on 429 with jittered exponential backoff.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete spec for how `Retry-After` is extracted on the bulk subprocess path (e.g., pattern-match `Retry-After: <N>` in `CalledProcessError.stderr`, or acknowledge it is unavailable and fall back to exponential backoff only), co-located with the `_run_acli` retry protocol in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan directs 'parse/honor Retry-After' in _run_acli but never defines the extraction mechanism: _run_acli raises subprocess.CalledProcessError (no .headers attribute), so the HTTP header is not directly accessible. The plan gives no protocol for how Retry-After would be sourced from ACLI subprocess output (e.g., scan stderr text, inspect ACLI --json stdout, or is it simply unavailable on this path). Without specifying the source, an implementer must guess whether to pattern-match stderr, parse JSON stdout, or treat the field as always-absent on the subprocess path.
findings.location: ## What / files — 'parse/honor Retry-After'; AC line: 'Bulk-path retries honor Retry-After on 429 with jittered exponential backoff.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Retry-After extraction protocol' subsection under '## What / files': specify that `CalledProcessError.stderr` is scanned for a `Retry-After: <seconds>` line (emitted by some reverse proxies / API gateways in ACLI stderr), that the parsed value overrides the computed base delay, and that any parse failure falls back to exponential backoff — mirroring the explicit fallback in `_call_with_backoff` (line 271-275). If ACLI does not emit this header, state so explicitly and note the AC should be updated to 'jittered exponential backoff only (Retry-After unavailable on subprocess path)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that there is no separate 'bulk path' — all subprocess ACLI calls (search, create, update, delete) share `_run_acli`; specify whether the fix should live inside `_run_acli`, in a new wrapper called by `_run_acli`, or at specific call sites, and document the blast radius on non-search/create operations.
findings.finding: The plan describes the ACLI bulk path as a distinct retry path separate from `_run_acli`, but in the codebase there is no separate 'bulk path' — `search_issues`, `create_issue`, and all other subprocess-ACLI operations route through the single `_run_acli` chokepoint. Any change to retry behavior on 'search/create' necessarily changes `_run_acli` globally, affecting ALL subprocess operations (update, delete, comment, link) — not just search/create.
findings.location: What / files section; plan prose: 'the ACLI-subprocess bulk path (search/create)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'bulk path (search/create)' with 'the `_run_acli` subprocess path' and note that the change affects all ACLI subprocess calls. Add a note on whether non-idempotent write paths (create, delete) need any special guard to prevent retry on ambiguous timeouts, cross-referencing AC4.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the narrowing to {429,502,503} is intentional (retire retry for other exit codes) or whether the plan means to ADD selective Retry-After/backoff logic for those codes ON TOP OF the existing broad retry, and document the intended behavior for currently-retried codes outside the set.
findings.finding: The plan says 'Retryable HTTP set matches the REST path {429,502,503}', but _run_acli currently retries ALL non-zero CalledProcessError exit codes (except 401 and assignee errors). Narrowing to only {429,502,503} would silently stop retrying other currently-retried transient failures (e.g. exit code 500, non-HTTP network errors surfacing as non-429 exits). This behavioral change is unacknowledged and could reduce robustness for failures outside the named set.
findings.location: AC line: 'Retryable HTTP set matches the REST path {429,502,503}'; acli_subprocess.py:428-431

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC4-mapped step (or unit test name) that explicitly verifies the no-blind-retry-on-timeout guard is preserved for non-idempotent bulk writes, e.g. a test that confirms a timed-out create is NOT retried.
findings.finding: AC4 ('Non-idempotent writes retain the no-blind-retry-on-timeout guard') has no mapped implementation step or test, so no described work reaches this criterion.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What/files bullet: 'Preserve the existing non-idempotent-write guard (no retry on timeout for create operations); add test `test_bulk_create_timeout_not_retried` to confirm.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a description in the What/files section of the existing no-blind-retry-on-timeout guard (location in code, its purpose) and explicitly state it must be preserved when refactoring the bulk-path retry loop.
findings.finding: AC4 ('Non-idempotent writes retain the no-blind-retry-on-timeout guard') has no corresponding described work in the What/files section — the plan body does not mention where this guard lives, what it looks like, or that it must be preserved during the refactor.
findings.location: AC line 4 vs. ## What / files section
findings.suggested_fix: In the What/files section, add: 'Preserve the existing guard that prevents blind retries on timeout for non-idempotent writes (currently at acli_subprocess.py:L<N>); the refactored retry loop must not apply Retry-After-backed retries to timed-out non-idempotent operations.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC (e.g. `pytest tests/test_acli_subprocess.py::test_bulk_path_429_retries_honor_retry_after -v` for AC3, a code-grep or assertion for AC2's retryable set, and a named test or assertion for AC4).
findings.finding: No AC has a concrete proving command — no pytest invocation, no test file path, no grep/assert command. An executor cannot verify completion without stopping to construct these.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to each AC a `Proven by:` line, e.g.: AC3 → `Proven by: pytest tests/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/test_acli_subprocess.py -k 'bulk_429' -v`; AC2 → `Proven by: grep -n '502\|503\|429' src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/acli_subprocess.py` plus a test assertion on the retryable set constant; AC4 → `Proven by: pytest ... -k 'no_blind_retry'`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define which bulk-path operations are 'non-idempotent' in the plan (e.g. 'create and upsert are non-idempotent; search is idempotent and always retryable') or add a pointer to the existing code comment that establishes this.
findings.finding: AC4 uses the undefined term 'non-idempotent writes' without specifying which bulk-path operations qualify, making it ambiguous whether search vs. create vs. upsert operations are in scope for the guard.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical: 'Non-idempotent writes (i.e. `bulk_create`, `bulk_upsert` — not `bulk_search`) retain the no-blind-retry-on-timeout guard.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback strategy (e.g., a cap on Retry-After wait time, a feature flag, or a documented 'revert commit X' procedure) for the case where honoring large Retry-After headers stalls bulk operations in production.
findings.finding: The plan changes existing bulk-path retry behavior (fixed delays → jittered exponential + Retry-After) without providing a rollback path or expand-contract sequence if the new behavior causes regressions in production (e.g., longer wait times due to large Retry-After values stalling pipelines).
findings.location: ## What / files and ## Acceptance Criteria sections
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback / Safety' section: cap honored Retry-After at a configurable maximum (e.g., 60 s, matching the REST path if one exists), and document that reverting the commit restores fixed-delay behavior; or introduce a flag `ACLI_HONOR_RETRY_AFTER=true` defaulting to true but overridable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the shared retryable HTTP set {429,502,503} and backoff constants into module-level constants (or a shared helper) so both the REST path and bulk path reference a single source of truth.
findings.finding: The retryable set {429,502,503} and backoff parameters will be duplicated in two places within `acli_subprocess.py` (REST path and bulk path). If these evolve, both sites must be updated in sync.
findings.location: AC line: 'Retryable HTTP set matches the REST path {429,502,503}' / `acli_subprocess.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Define `_RETRYABLE_STATUS_CODES = frozenset({429, 502, 503})` and shared backoff constants at module level in `acli_subprocess.py`; have both the REST retry logic and the new bulk-path retry logic reference them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or explicit implementation note) requiring that each bulk-path 429 retry logs the status code, Retry-After value, and computed backoff delay at an appropriate level (e.g., WARNING).
findings.finding: The plan does not specify that bulk-path 429 retries will be logged/instrumented, leaving operators blind to sustained rate-limiting on the bulk path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria / `acli_subprocess.py` — bulk path retry loop

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify which bulk-path operations are non-idempotent writes, document how the no-blind-retry-on-timeout guard changes their current retry behavior, and acknowledge any callers that relied on timeout retries being retried.
findings.finding: The plan adds a no-blind-retry-on-timeout guard to the bulk path (which currently retries all CalledProcessError generically, including timeouts), changing existing retry behavior for non-idempotent write callers without identifying which operations are affected or whether they depend on the current timeout-retry behavior.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Non-idempotent writes retain the no-blind-retry-on-timeout guard'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the plan naming the specific bulk-path operations (e.g. create) that are non-idempotent, confirm that losing timeout retries for them is acceptable/desired, and add a unit test asserting the guard fires correctly so the behavior change is observable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add unit test coverage (or extend the existing AC3 test) to assert that 502 and 503 responses on the bulk path also trigger retries, so all three members of the retryable set have observable proof.
findings.finding: The unit test AC covers only the 429/Retry-After scenario; there is no specified observable proof for 502/503 retryability on the bulk path, leaving two of the three retryable codes unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Unit test asserting a 429 on the bulk path backs off and honors Retry-After' and 'Retryable HTTP set matches the REST path {429,502,503}'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3 to: 'Unit tests asserting that 429 (with Retry-After), 502, and 503 responses on the bulk path all trigger retries with jittered exponential backoff.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC with a concrete operational validation mechanism (e.g., a staging integration test or smoke-test scenario showing that a simulated 429 storm on the bulk path self-heals within the expected backoff window, or an explicit note that existing monitoring metrics will confirm reduced retry errors post-deploy).
findings.finding: No concrete validation mechanism is specified for the reliability improvement: all four ACs are unit-test assertions or code-internal constraints, but none names an observable operational signal (e.g., reduced 429-storm duration in staging, retry-count metric, before/after error-rate comparison) that would confirm the bulk-path parity delivers its intended value in production.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'Integration/smoke test: when the bulk path receives 3 consecutive 429 responses with `Retry-After: 2`, the total elapsed time before success is ≥ 6 s and no request is issued earlier than the header-specified delay (verified by subprocess call timestamps in the test).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale explaining why `_run_acli` is the right location for the fix (e.g., it is the single dispatch chokepoint for all ACLI subprocess calls, ensuring all callers — reads and writes — inherit the improved behavior without per-call-site changes).
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for why modifying _run_acli directly (rather than, e.g., a call-site wrapper in acli_cli_ops.py that intercepts CalledProcessError for 429 codes) is the right approach. Per G6 rubric, absence of positive rationale is a finding.
findings.location: ## What / files section; plan body overall
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the '## What / files' section: '`_run_acli` is the correct chokepoint because it is the sole dispatch path for all ACLI subprocess invocations; fixing it here propagates the improved behavior to every caller (search, create, comment, graph ops) without per-call-site changes.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
